course.title: HTTP Protocol Engineering in Go 1.26: Bytes to HTTP/3
course.description: Go service developers who can build production HTTP clients and servers and now need byte-level protocol, parser, interoperability, and security mastery. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate go 1.26.5 octet and stream handling, partial i/o, tcp lifecycle, strict bounded http/1.1 parsing and serialization, request targets, fields, framing, fixed and chunked content, trailers, response control data, persistence, expectations, intermediaries, authority, tls 1.3, alpn, upgrade, connect, request smuggling and response splitting defense, slow-client limits, state machines, differential and fuzz testing, http/2 frames and hpack, http/3 quic and qpack, deployment, and incident defense through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate runnable deterministic pure-go protocol decision models plus explicit compilation, net.pipe, loopback socket, tls, proxy, packet-capture, fuzz, race, load, http/2, http/3, and production transfer gates through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
Demonstrate five cumulative protocol-engineering projects and a performance-based certification exam through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves Go, HTTP client and server, accessibility, security, testing, context, cleanup, and evidence habits before adding one protocol boundary.

Browser Go is deterministic and isolated. It models octets, framing, state, policy, and changed cases but never opens sockets, negotiates TLS, contacts networks or proxies, captures packets, executes host commands, reads host state, or handles credentials.

Passing work requires exact bytes or state input, a named invariant and owner, bounded resource and time policy, observable changed and failure results, cleanup and recovery, and explicit full-toolchain transfer limits.

competencies.statement: Define user, client, operator, security, accessibility, interoperability, and recovery evidence for a protocol implementation.
misconceptions: Successfully serving one browser request proves a protocol implementation is correct.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Define user, client, operator, security, accessibility, interoperability, and recovery evidence for a protocol implementation.

competencies.statement: Trace application policy, HTTP semantics, HTTP/1.1 syntax, TLS records, TCP byte streams, IP delivery, and local socket APIs without collapsing layers.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Trace application policy, HTTP semantics, HTTP/1.1 syntax, TLS records, TCP byte streams, IP delivery, and local socket APIs without collapsing layers.

competencies.statement: Use current RFC requirements, errata, Go documentation, and implementation tests as distinct authorities and resolve conflicts explicitly.

competencies.statement: Separate required conformance, optional tolerance, local policy, registered extensions, and unsupported behavior in parser decisions.
misconceptions: A parser is more interoperable when it accepts every malformed variation.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Separate required conformance, optional tolerance, local policy, registered extensions, and unsupported behavior in parser decisions.

competencies.statement: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compilation, net.Pipe tests, loopback sockets, TLS, packet capture, proxy, fuzz, race, load, and production transfer gates.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compilation, net.Pipe tests, loopback sockets, TLS, packet capture, proxy, fuzz, race, load, and production transfer gates.

competencies.statement: Complete bounded output across short writes while retaining progress, error, and ownership evidence.

competencies.statement: Use bufio.Reader without losing peeked, unread, or already-buffered bytes when protocol phases change.

competencies.statement: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

competencies.statement: Treat failed or timed-out writes as potentially partially observed and avoid blind replay of non-idempotent protocol effects.

competencies.statement: Validate Connection upgrade, Upgrade tokens, request completion, status 101, selected protocol, and ownership transfer before switching parsers.
misconceptions: An Upgrade request authorizes the client to send the new protocol immediately.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Validate Connection upgrade, Upgrade tokens, request completion, status 101, selected protocol, and ownership transfer before switching parsers.

competencies.statement: Parse CONNECT authority-form, validate host and port policy, authenticate proxy use, establish the target, then transition after a successful response.
misconceptions: CONNECT may target any supplied host and port because the server only transports bytes.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Parse CONNECT authority-form, validate host and port policy, authenticate proxy use, establish the target, then transition after a successful response.

competencies.statement: Transfer connection, buffered unread octets, deadlines, close ownership, and cancellation atomically to the selected protocol or tunnel.
misconceptions: Changing parser functions is enough; bufio cannot hold bytes beyond the HTTP message.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Transfer connection, buffered unread octets, deadlines, close ownership, and cancellation atomically to the selected protocol or tunnel.

competencies.statement: Apply RFC 9931 by forbidding unsafe optimistic new-protocol bytes in HTTP/1.1 until transition acceptance is known.
misconceptions: Completing the transition request guarantees the server accepted the new protocol.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Apply RFC 9931 by forbidding unsafe optimistic new-protocol bytes in HTTP/1.1 until transition acceptance is known.

competencies.statement: Continue HTTP/1.1 safely after a rejected transition only when buffered bytes, framing, peer behavior, and parser state remain unambiguous.

competencies.statement: Reject Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length and conflicting length signals, consume no ambiguous next message, and close the connection.
misconceptions: CL.TE and TE.CL ambiguity is harmless when the local parser chooses one framing rule consistently.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Reject Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length and conflicting length signals, consume no ambiguous next message, and close the connection.

competencies.statement: Reject or consistently handle bare line endings, whitespace before colons, obsolete folding, leading blank lines, and invalid request-line separators across every hop.
misconceptions: Normalizing malformed whitespace before forwarding removes all request-smuggling risk.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Reject or consistently handle bare line endings, whitespace before colons, obsolete folding, leading blank lines, and invalid request-line separators across every hop.

competencies.statement: Compare raw and decoded targets, path separators, percent encodings, authority, query boundaries, and routing between policy and downstream recipients.
misconceptions: If two parsers produce the same visible path, they necessarily enforce the same request boundary.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Compare raw and decoded targets, path separators, percent encodings, authority, query boundaries, and routing between policy and downstream recipients.

competencies.statement: Prevent attacker-controlled CRLF and control bytes from creating new fields, responses, logs, caches, or downstream protocol records.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Prevent attacker-controlled CRLF and control bytes from creating new fields, responses, logs, caches, or downstream protocol records.

competencies.statement: Test HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 to HTTP/1.1 translation, proxy chains, cache parsing, and backend normalization with the same adversarial corpus.
misconceptions: A safe HTTP/2 front end guarantees the translated HTTP/1.1 backend request cannot be ambiguous.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Test HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 to HTTP/1.1 translation, proxy chains, cache parsing, and backend normalization with the same adversarial corpus.

competencies.statement: Bound start-line, header, content, trailer, idle, and total-phase time using both progress and absolute budgets.
misconceptions: Refreshing a read deadline after every byte defeats slow-client resource exhaustion.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Bound start-line, header, content, trailer, idle, and total-phase time using both progress and absolute budgets.

competencies.statement: Bound per-connection buffers, retained slices, field maps, content queues, trailer data, and pooled-object capacity before allocation.

competencies.statement: Propagate slow peer writes through bounded queues, cancellation, deadlines, partial output evidence, and producer shutdown.

competencies.statement: Limit connections, active parsers, work, tenant share, and output while rejecting early and preserving fairness and health evidence.

competencies.statement: Prove connection, goroutine, timer, buffer, queue, tunnel, temporary data, and metric-cardinality cleanup for every terminal state.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Prove connection, goroutine, timer, buffer, queue, tunnel, temporary data, and metric-cardinality cleanup for every terminal state.

competencies.statement: Represent protocol phase, required input, transition, emitted event, retained buffer, limit, and terminal result explicitly across fragmented reads.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Run race, leak, allocation, benchmark, profiling, and changed-load gates while separating correctness from capacity claims.

competencies.statement: Apply idle, open, half-closed, closed, reserved, reset, and connection shutdown transitions independently per stream.
misconceptions: Closing one HTTP/2 stream closes the underlying TCP connection and every other stream.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Apply idle, open, half-closed, closed, reserved, reset, and connection shutdown transitions independently per stream.

misconceptions: HPACK compression is stateless per request and cannot expose cross-stream information.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Map pseudo-fields and regular fields, enforce ordering and connection-field rules, bound HPACK tables and header lists, and avoid indexing secrets.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Trace HTTP/3 control streams, SETTINGS, request streams, frame placement, cancellation, GOAWAY, and connection versus stream errors.

credential.title: HTTP Protocol Engineering in Go 1.26: Bytes to HTTP/3 Certificate

modules.title: Protocol Outcomes, Layers, Standards, and Evidence
modules.description: A team says it built HTTP from scratch because one request works, but it cannot separate application semantics, wire syntax, transport behavior, implementation policy, or user and operator evidence. Learners cumulatively produce protocol outcome, layer, authority, and evidence charter through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define user, client, operator, security, accessibility, interoperability, and recovery evidence for a protocol implementation.
Trace application policy, HTTP semantics, HTTP/1.1 syntax, TLS records, TCP byte streams, IP delivery, and local socket APIs without collapsing layers.
Use current RFC requirements, errata, Go documentation, and implementation tests as distinct authorities and resolve conflicts explicitly.

modules.title: Octets, Byte Streams, Fragmentation, Buffering, and Partial I/O
modules.description: A parser assumes one Read returns a complete line, converts arbitrary bytes to text too early, loses buffered bytes, and treats a short read or write as end-of-message. Learners cumulatively produce fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Keep protocol data as octets until grammar permits text interpretation and distinguish ASCII tokens, opaque content, and invalid control bytes.
Interpret Read results using both byte count and error, including data with io.EOF, zero-byte reads, and temporary fragmentation.
Complete bounded output across short writes while retaining progress, error, and ownership evidence.

modules.title: TCP Connections, Accept Loops, Half-Closes, Deadlines, and Failure
modules.description: A raw server treats TCP as messages, leaks accepted connections, uses no deadlines, retries ambiguous writes, and cannot distinguish peer close, timeout, reset, or local shutdown. Learners cumulatively produce bounded TCP connection lifecycle state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Explain ordered reliable byte-stream delivery while accounting for segmentation, retransmission, flow control, congestion, and missing message boundaries.
Assign listener, accepted connection, goroutine, buffer, deadline, and close ownership with bounded accept-error handling.
Set and refresh absolute read and write deadlines by protocol phase and classify timeout evidence without assuming cancellation.

modules.title: HTTP Semantics, Messages, Versions, and the Wire Model
modules.description: A raw implementation copies endpoint behavior into its parser, confuses resources with representations, infers message bodies from method names, and treats HTTP/1.1 syntax as universal HTTP semantics. Learners cumulatively produce version-neutral message and semantics model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Separate version-neutral method, status, field, content, and resource semantics from HTTP/1.1 textual serialization.
Model control data, header fields, content, trailer fields, and message metadata without inventing content where semantics forbid it.
Determine content meaning and permission from request method, response status, request context, and connection mode rather than one field alone.

modules.title: ABNF, Tokens, Whitespace, Line Endings, Limits, and Parser Architecture
modules.description: A parser splits strings freely, accepts bare line feeds and whitespace before fields, has no byte limits, and mixes syntax recognition with application side effects. Learners cumulatively produce strict bounded incremental parser architecture through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Translate RFC ABNF alternatives, repetition, terminals, lists, and prose constraints into explicit parser states and tests.
Validate tokens and visible bytes using octet classes while rejecting separators, controls, NUL, and forbidden whitespace.
Require CRLF at HTTP/1.1 structural boundaries and document any local robustness exception without parser disagreement.

modules.title: Request Lines, Methods, Target Forms, Versions, and Routing Inputs
modules.description: A parser uses Fields on the request line, normalizes targets before validation, treats every target as origin-form, and accepts invalid CONNECT authorities and absolute-form credentials. Learners cumulatively produce strict request-line and request-target parser through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Parse method, one-space delimiters, request target, HTTP version, and CRLF without accepting ambiguous extra components.
Recognize any valid method token, preserve case, then apply supported-method policy separately.
Distinguish origin-form, absolute-form, authority-form, and asterisk-form using method and recipient role.

modules.title: Field Lines, Repetition, Combination, Structured Fields, and Limits
modules.description: A parser stores one value per name, splits every comma, accepts obsolete folding, merges Set-Cookie, and treats all field values as trusted UTF-8 text. Learners cumulatively produce loss-aware bounded field-section model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Parse case-insensitive token field names, colon delimiters, optional field whitespace, and field values while rejecting whitespace before the colon.
Preserve field-line order and repeated values, combining only when that field definition permits it.
Parse Connection options and nominate hop-by-hop fields dynamically instead of relying only on a fixed list.

modules.title: HTTP/1.1 Message Framing and Length Precedence
modules.description: A recipient reads until EOF for every body, trusts duplicate Content-Length values, accepts Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length, and makes a framing decision before status and method semantics are known. Learners cumulatively produce RFC 9112 message-body length decision table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Apply the RFC 9112 message-body length precedence in order using request or response role, method, status, transfer coding, content length, and connection closure.
Forbid content framing for HEAD responses, 1xx, 204, successful CONNECT, and other context-specific cases while retaining metadata meaning.
Validate transfer-coding order, require final chunked coding for request framing, and close after ambiguous Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length input.

modules.title: Fixed-Length, Close-Delimited, Absent, and Incomplete Content
modules.description: A body reader allocates from attacker Content-Length, treats premature EOF as success, consumes the next pipelined message, and reuses a connection after incomplete content. Learners cumulatively produce bounded fixed-length and close-delimited content reader through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Stream exactly the validated content length through independent policy limits without allocating the declared size.
Classify EOF before the required octet count as incomplete framing and prevent dispatch or connection reuse.
Stop after exactly the framed content and retain buffered residual bytes for the next message.

modules.title: Chunked Transfer Coding, Extensions, Trailers, and Decoding
modules.description: A decoder parses chunk sizes as decimal, trusts extensions, misses overflow, forgets the zero chunk, merges forbidden trailers into headers, and leaves bytes misaligned. Learners cumulatively produce incremental chunked decoder and trailer policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Parse bounded hexadecimal chunk size with extensions, overflow checks, exact CRLF, and cumulative decoded limits.
Read exactly each chunk-data length plus its terminating CRLF across arbitrary fragmentation.
Treat the last zero-sized chunk as the transition to trailer parsing rather than immediate message completion.

modules.title: Status Lines, Informational Responses, Content Rules, and Response Parsing
modules.description: A client parser accepts malformed status codes, stops at 100 Continue, assigns content to HEAD and 204, treats successful CONNECT as ordinary HTTP, and retries after an incomplete response. Learners cumulatively produce response control-data and content decision parser through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Parse HTTP version, three-digit status code, optional reason phrase, and CRLF while treating the reason phrase as non-authoritative.
Process zero or more permitted informational responses before the final response with bounded count and protocol-specific handling.
Parse HEAD response metadata as if GET were selected while never consuming response content bytes.

modules.title: HTTP/1.1 Response Serialization, Field Order, Framing, and Writes
modules.description: A writer emits content before headers, injects unchecked field values, chooses conflicting framing, assumes one Write completes, and cannot prove exact transmitted bytes. Learners cumulatively produce canonical bounded HTTP/1.1 response serializer through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Commit one status line, validated field section, empty line, framed content, and trailers in protocol order.
Reject CR, LF, NUL, controls, invalid names, and unsafe values before serializing any response field.
Choose no-content, fixed-length, chunked, or close-delimited output from method, status, known length, streaming, version, and reuse policy.

modules.title: Persistent Connections, Pipelining, Ordering, Reuse, and Closure
modules.description: A server dispatches the next request before consuming the current body, writes responses out of order, reuses connections after framing errors, and waits forever for idle peers. Learners cumulatively produce persistent-connection request and response state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Determine HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 persistence from version, Connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy.
Consume, reject-and-close, or deliberately drain the current framed request before parsing a subsequent request.
Preserve request-response ordering across pipelined HTTP/1.1 messages even when application work finishes out of order.

modules.title: Expect 100-continue, Early Responses, Body Rejection, and Draining
modules.description: A server reads a huge body before authentication, sends 100 after a final response, drains attacker bytes without bounds, and lets client and server wait on each other forever. Learners cumulatively produce expectation and early-response coordination state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Parse Expect, support 100-continue deliberately, and return appropriate final failure for unsupported expectations.
Send 100 Continue only after header-level policy accepts receiving content and before a final response commits.
Bound client waiting for 100, handle early final responses, and avoid sending content after rejection.

modules.title: Intermediaries, Hop-by-Hop Fields, Forwarding, and Transformation
modules.description: A proxy forwards Connection-nominated fields, copies raw forwarding claims, changes content without metadata repair, and assumes downstream and upstream share one connection and parser. Learners cumulatively produce intermediary forwarding and transformation contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Remove Connection and every nominated hop-by-hop field before forwarding, then establish fields for the new hop.
Model downstream and upstream connections, framing, versions, deadlines, addresses, TLS, and failures as independent protocol hops.
Update framing, validators, digests, content codings, warnings, and signatures when an intermediary transforms content.

modules.title: Request Authority, Host, Target Reconstruction, and Routing
modules.description: A server trusts any Host, accepts duplicate authority sources, reconstructs links from forwarded input, routes before validating the target, and confuses TLS identity with request authority. Learners cumulatively produce authority validation and target reconstruction table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Require exactly one valid Host field for HTTP/1.1 where applicable and reject missing, empty, duplicate, or invalid authority.
Reconstruct the effective request URI from target form, request-target, Host, connection security, and trusted configuration.
Handle proxy absolute-form authority and Host according to RFC rules while rejecting unsafe disagreement.

modules.title: TLS 1.3, Certificate Identity, ALPN, and HTTPS
modules.description: A service enables TLS without certificate-name validation, chooses HTTP after reading encrypted application bytes, allows unsafe versions, and treats encryption as authorization and content integrity proof. Learners cumulatively produce TLS identity, negotiation, and protocol boundary record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish TCP connection, TLS records, handshake, authenticated peer identity, traffic keys, alerts, application data, and closure.
Validate certificate chain, time, DNS or IP reference identity, key usage, revocation policy, and trust roots without insecure skip flags.
Offer and select application protocols with ALPN before application data and reject unsupported or inconsistent negotiation.

modules.title: Upgrade, CONNECT, Tunnels, WebSocket Boundaries, and Safe Transitions
modules.description: A client sends new-protocol bytes before acceptance, a server parses tunneled data as another HTTP request, buffered bytes disappear during handoff, and rejected transitions desynchronize the connection. Learners cumulatively produce confirmed protocol-transition and buffered-byte handoff state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Validate Connection upgrade, Upgrade tokens, request completion, status 101, selected protocol, and ownership transfer before switching parsers.
Parse CONNECT authority-form, validate host and port policy, authenticate proxy use, establish the target, then transition after a successful response.
Transfer connection, buffered unread octets, deadlines, close ownership, and cancellation atomically to the selected protocol or tunnel.

modules.title: Request Smuggling, Response Splitting, Parser Differentials, and Downgrades
modules.description: A front end and origin disagree on length, whitespace, duplicate fields, target decoding, and line endings; the team tests each parser alone and forwards ambiguous messages after normalization. Learners cumulatively produce multi-recipient parser-consistency threat model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Reject Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length and conflicting length signals, consume no ambiguous next message, and close the connection.
Reject or consistently handle bare line endings, whitespace before colons, obsolete folding, leading blank lines, and invalid request-line separators across every hop.
Compare raw and decoded targets, path separators, percent encodings, authority, query boundaries, and routing between policy and downstream recipients.

modules.title: Slow Clients, Resource Limits, Backpressure, Admission, and Cleanup
modules.description: A server gives every connection a goroutine and unlimited buffer, refreshes deadlines on single-byte trickles, queues output without bounds, and retries accept failures until the process collapses. Learners cumulatively produce connection resource budget and overload policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Bound start-line, header, content, trailer, idle, and total-phase time using both progress and absolute budgets.
Bound per-connection buffers, retained slices, field maps, content queues, trailer data, and pooled-object capacity before allocation.
Propagate slow peer writes through bounded queues, cancellation, deadlines, partial output evidence, and producer shutdown.

modules.title: Incremental State Machines, Concurrency, Cancellation, and Ownership
modules.description: A parser is recursive and side-effectful, multiple goroutines read one connection, cancellation races with response writes, and error paths cannot state which component owns the buffer or close. Learners cumulatively produce explicit protocol state, event, invariant, and ownership model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Represent protocol phase, required input, transition, emitted event, retained buffer, limit, and terminal result explicitly across fragmented reads.
Define invariants for cursor bounds, monotonic consumption, one framing choice, one response order, one close owner, and no effects before validation.
Assign one coordinated reader and ordered writer per HTTP/1.1 connection with explicit handoff for tunnels and shutdown.

modules.title: Golden Transcripts, net.Pipe, Differential Tests, Fuzzing, Race, and Benchmarks
modules.description: Tests send one complete request through loopback, compare only status, reuse the implementation as its own oracle, fuzz without invariants, and publish benchmark throughput without fragmentation or adversarial peers. Learners cumulatively produce layered deterministic protocol verification system through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use exact byte fixtures for valid, incomplete, malformed, normalized, serialized, and residual-buffer cases with readable semantic assertions.
Use net.Pipe or a scripted short reader and writer to test fragmentation, blocking, deadlines, half-close, cancellation, and cleanup deterministically.
Compare supported inputs with Go net/http and independent recipients while treating deliberate strictness and implementation quirks as reviewed differences.

modules.title: HTTP/2 Frames, Streams, HPACK, Flow Control, and Go Boundaries
modules.description: A team assumes HTTP/2 is compressed HTTP/1.1 text, shares one parser state across streams, ignores SETTINGS and flow control, indexes secrets into HPACK, and maps every stream failure to connection failure. Learners cumulatively produce HTTP/2 connection, stream, frame, and compression model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace connection preface, SETTINGS exchange and acknowledgement, frame header, payload length, type, flags, stream identifier, and connection errors.
Apply idle, open, half-closed, closed, reserved, reset, and connection shutdown transitions independently per stream.
Map pseudo-fields and regular fields, enforce ordering and connection-field rules, bound HPACK tables and header lists, and avoid indexing secrets.

modules.title: HTTP/3, QUIC, QPACK, Migration, and Production Protocol Defense
modules.description: A release claims HTTP/3 because UDP is open, ignores QUIC connection identity and migration, treats QPACK like HPACK, advertises Alt-Svc without rollback, and cannot defend parser, TLS, proxy, or capacity evidence. Learners cumulatively produce multi-version protocol release and incident defense through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish QUIC connection IDs, encrypted packets, transport parameters, bidirectional and unidirectional streams, migration, loss recovery, and UDP path behavior.
Trace HTTP/3 control streams, SETTINGS, request streams, frame placement, cancellation, GOAWAY, and connection versus stream errors.
Explain QPACK encoder and decoder streams, dynamic-table capacity, blocked streams, acknowledgements, confidentiality, and decompression limits.
